critic_raw:
The most fundamental flaw in this design is the use of stratified random splitting on time-series IoT sensor data. The design explicitly frames the justification as maintaining 'cross-sectional signal integrity,' but equipment failures in manufacturing are not cross-sectional events — they arise from degradation trajectories that unfold over time. A random split will scatter temporally adjacent readings across train/test boundaries, meaning the model can be evaluated on measurements taken between two training-set readings from the same degradation curve. This constitutes a form of temporal leakage that inflates anomaly detection performance, because patterns immediately surrounding a failure event will appear in training even if the evaluation point is nominally held out.

Second, the labeling scheme — 'significant deviations from normal operational patterns' — is circular when the CNN is the model being evaluated. If anomaly labels are derived from deviation from a baseline normal, and the CNN is tested on anomaly detection, the label generation process effectively pre-encodes the signal the model is expected to learn. The design does not clarify whether labels were produced by an independent process (e.g., confirmed maintenance records, physical inspection logs) or by statistical thresholding on the same sensor streams the CNN processes. If the latter, the comparison with a threshold-based system is tautological.

Third, the primary metric — reduction in false positive rate — is an incomplete framing for anomaly detection. Without fixing a corresponding recall or miss rate, reducing FPR to zero is trivially achieved by predicting no anomalies. The design does not specify at what operating point the FPR reduction is measured, nor does it define a decision threshold strategy for converting CNN outputs to binary alerts. This means the reported metric can be gamed by threshold choice post-hoc.

Fourth, the threshold-based baseline is described as 'tuned to match the complexity permitted for each choice,' which is vague. If the threshold baseline is tuned on the test set or on the same data used to derive anomaly labels, it is not a fair comparison. The design provides no specifics on how threshold tuning is conducted independently.

Fifth, the claim that CNNs capture 'spatial hierarchies' in time-series data conflates the architectural motivation for image CNNs (2D spatial locality) with 1D temporal locality. While 1D CNNs do capture local temporal patterns, describing this as 'spatial hierarchy' suggests a misunderstanding of the architectural advantage being claimed, which raises questions about whether the architecture was actually chosen for the right reasons or whether a simpler architecture (e.g., sliding-window features with gradient boosting) would match it.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on time-series sensor data creates temporal leakage: degradation trajectories are continuous, so adjacent readings from the same failure sequence can appear in both train and test, inflating measured anomaly detection performance.

Anomaly labels defined as 'significant deviations from normal operational patterns' are circular if derived from the same sensor streams the CNN processes, making the comparison with a threshold baseline potentially tautological rather than a fair head-to-head.

The primary metric — reduction in false positive rate — is undefined without a fixed operating point or recall constraint; it can be trivially minimized by suppressing all alerts, making the metric uninterpretable without specifying the miss-rate budget.
The threshold-based baseline tuning procedure is not specified; if thresholds are optimized on the same data used for evaluation or on label-derived statistics, the comparison is not fair.

Claiming CNNs capture 'spatial hierarchies' in time-series data misappropriates image-domain motivation; the actual advantage (local temporal pattern recognition) is modest and may not exceed simpler sliding-window tabular baselines that are absent from the comparison.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split on time-series sensor data creates temporal leakage: degradation trajectories are continuous, so adjacent readings from the same failure sequence can appear in both train and test, inflating measured anomaly detection performance.

Anomaly labels defined as 'significant deviations from normal operational patterns' are circular if derived from the same sensor streams the CNN processes, making the comparison with a threshold baseline potentially tautological rather than a fair head-to-head.

The primary metric — reduction in false positive rate — is undefined without a fixed operating point or recall constraint; it can be trivially minimized by suppressing all alerts, making the metric uninterpretable without specifying the miss-rate budget.
The threshold-based baseline tuning procedure is not specified; if thresholds are optimized on the same data used for evaluation or on label-derived statistics, the comparison is not fair.

Claiming CNNs capture 'spatial hierarchies' in time-series data misappropriates image-domain motivation; the actual advantage (local temporal pattern recognition) is modest and may not exceed simpler sliding-window tabular baselines that are absent from the comparison.
